content:
Generative modeling predates the "Generative AI" product label by decades. **Hidden Markov models** and **n-gram language models** (20th century) already assigned probabilities to sequences and could sample text—generative behavior with shallow statistical structure.

**Boltzmann machines**, **RBMs**, and early **VAEs** brought latent-variable generation into neural networks. **GANs** (2014) popularized adversarial training for sharp image synthesis. **Transformers** (2017) and **large-scale self-supervised pretraining** (2018–2020s) made general-purpose text and multimodal generation practical at consumer scale.

The term **Generative AI** rose in industry circa 2022–2023 as chat and image products reached mass adoption. In this curriculum, **Classical ML framing** records where that wave sits relative to AI, ML, and DL; the **Gen AI Fundamentals** stage covers model families, history, and LLM mechanics in detail—linked, not duplicated here.

timeline.label: Statistical language models
timeline.description: N-grams and HMMs sample sequences from estimated distributions—early generative behavior without deep nets.

timeline.label: Neural generative models
timeline.description: VAEs and GANs learn high-dimensional generators; deep learning becomes the default substrate for vision and audio synthesis.

timeline.label: Transformers + self-supervision
timeline.description: Attention-based models pretrained on vast unlabeled corpora enable flexible conditional generation (text, code, multimodal).

timeline.label: Gen AI product era
timeline.description: Instruction-tuned chat and diffusion image tools make "Generative AI" a mainstream category distinct from generic "AI" marketing.
